subject: Michael Milken — the 'junk bond king' of 1980s Wall Street: builder of the high-yield market, record earner, convicted felon, philanthropist, and 2020 presidential pardon recipient
central_question: Was Michael Milken a financial genius the system punished for winning too well, or a criminal the system was simply rich and connected enough to forgive — and why has no court, fortune, or pardon ever truly settled it?
viewer_promise: By the end, the viewer understands the idea that made him (high-yield 'junk' bonds), the empire and the record pay, exactly what he was CHARGED with (98 counts) versus what he PLEADED guilty to (6 felonies), the real shape of the punishment and the 2020 pardon, and why the verdict is handed deliberately to the viewer.
surprise: Most people 'remember' Milken as convicted of insider trading or racketeering at trial. He never went to trial: he pleaded guilty to six felonies, the RICO and 92 other counts were dropped, and his 2020 pardon forgave the crime without erasing the plea or lifting his lifetime SEC ban.
stakes: A record ~$550M single-year payday, a ~$600M penalty, a lifetime industry ban, a cancer prognosis of months that he outlived by decades, billions in philanthropy, and a pardon that split the verdict on him in two.
target_audience: English-speaking general audience that loves true-crime, Wall Street/finance stories, fraud and white-collar sagas, and morally complex character studies
demand_evidence: Wall Street greed / 1980s excess is a durable, high-search genre; Milken is its single most iconic figure and a recurring reference point in finance media.
The 2020 presidential pardon revived mainstream interest and gives the story a modern, debate-driving hook (clemency vs. innocence).
competition: Heavy coverage exists, but much of it repeats the myth ('convicted of insider trading/RICO at trial') or picks a side; few neutrally separate CHARGED vs PLEADED, explain the actual idea, and hand the verdict to the viewer.
differentiation: Treats Milken as a genuinely two-sided figure: a clear, plain-English explanation of the junk-bond insight, a disciplined CHARGED-vs-PLEADED legal spine, the punishment/pardon distinction (clemency is not innocence; the SEC ban stands), and an engineered 'you decide' ending. Record-based, neutral, advertiser-safe; symbolic visuals with no real-person likeness.
research_feasibility: High: the indictment, the 1990 guilty plea, the SEC settlement/lifetime bar, and the Feb 18, 2020 White House pardon statement are all on the public record, with extensive reputable reporting and two canonical books (Stewart's 'Den of Thieves', Bruck's 'The Predators' Ball').
visual_feasibility: High: symbolic finance/legal imagery — an X-shaped trading desk, vaults, yield curves, a 'highly confident' letter, an indictment stack collapsing to six pages, a pardon parchment beside a still-locked ban — all achievable with no real-person likeness, no real logos, and no readable text.
risk_notes: LIVING person. Strictly record-based and neutral. NEVER say 'convicted at trial', 'convicted of insider trading', or 'convicted of racketeering/RICO' — he pleaded guilty to SIX felony counts and the case never went to trial.
Always distinguish CHARGED (98-count indictment, 1989, incl. RICO) from PLEADED (6 felonies, April 1990). Charges are accusations, not findings; RICO + the other 92 counts were dropped.
The 2020 pardon is clemency, NOT innocence or exoneration; the guilty plea STANDS and the pardon did NOT lift the SEC lifetime industry ban.
The genius-vs-greed debate must be ATTRIBUTED ('critics argue / defenders argue'), never stated as settled fact in narration.
No real-person likeness in any AI visual (invariant 11); no real logos/seals; no readable text. Generated visuals are symbolic reconstruction, never presented as authentic records.
kill_conditions: Cannot hold the CHARGED-vs-PLEADED and pardon-is-not-innocence distinctions cleanly throughout the script.
Cannot keep the genius-vs-greed framing attributed and neutral for a living, pardoned person (defamation/neutrality risk).
